full_response:
Step 1 – Set-up and notation  
• Use units with c = 1 unless explicitly written.  
• Alice’s inertial coordinates (t, x, y, z) satisfy the flat metric  

  ds² = –dt² + dx² + dy² + dz² , x ≡ x + L .  

• Barbara moves with constant 3-velocity  

  v⃗ = v x̂ , |v| = v < 1 .  

Because the x–direction is periodically identified, as soon as Barbara’s x–coordinate has increased by L she is again at Alice’s spatial position.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2 – Time elapsed in Alice’s frame  
In Alice’s frame Barbara covers a coordinate distance Δx = L at speed v, so the coordinate time that elapses on Alice’s clock is  

  Δt_A = Δx / v = L / v . (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3 – Alice’s prediction for Barbara’s proper time (time dilation)  
For uniform motion the differential relation between coordinate time and proper time is  

  dτ_B = dt / γ , γ ≡ 1/√(1 – v²) .  

Integrating over the interval (1) gives  

  Δτ_B = Δt_A / γ  
     = (L / v) / γ  
     = L /(γ v)  
     = L / v · √(1 – v²) . (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary  

• Time that elapses on Alice’s clock between departure and reunion:  

  Δt_A = L / v .  

• Alice’s prediction for the time that elapses on Barbara’s clock:  

  Δτ_B = (L / γ v) = (L / v)√(1 – v²/c²) .  

(The result reproduces the usual twin-paradox time dilation; the compact topology singles out Alice’s frame, so no acceleration is required to break the symmetry.)

\boxed{\;\Delta t_{\text{Alice}} = \dfrac{L}{v}, \qquad
\Delta \tau_{\text{Barbara}} = \dfrac{L}{\gamma v} = \dfrac{L}{v}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}\;}

Confidence: 93%
